children.name: Rostratula
children.description: Medium-sized waders with beautifully patterned plumage that resembles true snipes but with a noticeably thicker, shorter bill. Like buttonquail, painted-snipes exhibit reversed sexual dimorphism — females are larger and more brightly coloured, competing for males who handle all incubation and chick-rearing. They are secretive marsh-dwellers, more often heard than seen.

children.name: Rostratula benghalensis
children.description: The most widespread painted-snipe, found across sub-Saharan Africa, Madagascar, and South and Southeast Asia. The female has a striking chestnut, white and buff pattern with a dark central crown stripe; the male is duller. It inhabits marshes, rice paddies, and wet grasslands, foraging at dawn and dusk.

children.name: Rostratula semicollaris
children.description: Endemic to southern South America, ranging from Peru and Brazil south to central Argentina and Chile. It is distinguished by a bold white eye-stripe and a whitish collar across the hindneck. It prefers freshwater marshes and bogs, remaining hidden in dense vegetation.

children.name: Rostratula australis
children.description: A rare and declining species endemic to Australia, formerly treated as a subspecies of the Greater Painted-snipe. The female has a distinctive horseshoe-shaped breast pattern and a dark hood. It is listed as endangered nationally, threatened by wetland drainage and changes in water management.
